# Noise Generators — Hypno 2

Procedural noise is the foundation of all Hypno 2 generative visuals. Everything from clouds to lava lamps to organic textures builds on noise functions. This guide covers noise types optimized for the Raspberry Pi 5 GPU.

## Core Principles

**Noise = pseudo-random, spatially continuous signal** generated by:
1. Hash function → random values at integer lattice points
2. Interpolation → smooth blending between lattice values
3. FBM → multi-octave summation for detail at multiple scales

## Hash Functions

Map integer coordinates to pseudo-random floats. Use **sin-free** variants for cross-platform stability on ARM GPU.

### Sin-Free Hash (Dave Hoskins — Recommended)

```glsl
float hash12(vec2 p) {
    vec3 p3 = fract(vec3(p.xyx) * 0.1031);
    p3 += dot(p3, p3.yzx + 33.33);
    return fract((p3.x + p3.y) * p3.z);
}

vec2 hash22(vec2 p) {
    vec3 p3 = fract(vec3(p.xyx) * vec3(0.1031, 0.1030, 0.0973));
    p3 += dot(p3, p3.yzx + 33.33);
    return fract((p3.xx + p3.yz) * p3.zy);
}
```

### Minimal Hash (fastest, good enough for most uses)

```glsl
float hash(vec2 p) {
    p = fract(p * 0.6180339887);  // golden ratio perturbation
    p *= 25.0;
    return fract(p.x * p.y * (p.x + p.y));
}
```

## Value Noise

Hash at integer lattice, smoothly interpolate.

```glsl
float noise(vec2 p) {
    vec2 i = floor(p);
    vec2 f = fract(p);
    // Hermite smoothstep
    f = f * f * (3.0 - 2.0 * f);
    
    float a = hash(i + vec2(0.0, 0.0));
    float b = hash(i + vec2(1.0, 0.0));
    float c = hash(i + vec2(0.0, 1.0));
    float d = hash(i + vec2(1.0, 1.0));
    
    return mix(mix(a, b, f.x), mix(c, d, f.x), f.y);
}
```

## FBM (Fractal Brownian Motion)

Sum noise at increasing frequencies with decreasing amplitudes. This is the workhorse.

### Standard FBM (clouds, smooth textures)

```glsl
// Rotation matrix for decorrelation (~36.87°)
const mat2 mtx = mat2(0.80, 0.60, -0.60, 0.80);

float fbm(vec2 p) {
    float f = 0.0;
    f += 0.500000 * noise(p); p = mtx * p * 2.02;
    f += 0.250000 * noise(p); p = mtx * p * 2.03;
    f += 0.125000 * noise(p); p = mtx * p * 2.01;
    f += 0.062500 * noise(p); p = mtx * p * 2.04;
    f += 0.031250 * noise(p); p = mtx * p * 2.01;
    f += 0.015625 * noise(p);
    return f / 0.96875;  // normalize to ~0-1
}
```

**Why varying lacunarity (2.01, 2.03, 2.01, 2.04)?**  
Exact 2.0× doubling creates visible grid artifacts. Slight variation breaks up lattice regularity. The rotation matrix (`mtx`) further decorrelates octaves.

### 4-Octave FBM (lighter weight for Pi 5)

```glsl
float fbm4(vec2 p) {
    float f = 0.0;
    f += 0.5000 * noise(p); p = mtx * p * 2.02;
    f += 0.2500 * noise(p); p = mtx * p * 2.03;
    f += 0.1250 * noise(p); p = mtx * p * 2.01;
    f += 0.0625 * noise(p);
    return f / 0.9375;
}
```

## FBM Variants

### Ridged FBM (mountains, lava, lightning)

```glsl
float fbm_ridged(vec2 p) {
    float z = 2.0, rz = 0.0;
    for (float i = 1.0; i < 6.0; i++) {
        // abs() creates V-shaped ridges at zero crossings
        rz += abs((noise(p) - 0.5) * 2.0) / z;
        z *= 2.0;
        p *= 2.0;
    }
    return rz;
}

// Sinusoidal ridged variant (lava flow texture)
// rz += (sin(noise(p) * 7.0) * 0.5 + 0.5) / z;
```

### Worley-Style Turbulence (cellular noise feel, cheaper than true Voronoi)

```glsl
float turbulence(vec2 p) {
    float t = 0.0;
    float a = 0.5;
    for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
        t += a * abs(noise(p) - 0.5) * 2.0;
        p *= 2.0;
        a *= 0.5;
    }
    return t;
}
```

## Time Animation in Noise

Inject `time` into noise coordinates for smooth motion. Two patterns:

### Global Flow (slow, sweeping)
```glsl
float animated_fbm(vec2 p, float time) {
    // Shift entire noise field slowly
    return fbm(p + vec2(time * 0.1, time * 0.05));
}
```

### Multi-Scale Motion (different speeds per octave)
```glsl
float animated_fbm(vec2 p, float time) {
    float f = 0.0;
    f += 0.5000 * noise(p + time * 0.1);             // slow overall
    p = mtx * p * 2.02;
    f += 0.2500 * noise(p);                           // static mid
    p = mtx * p * 2.03;
    f += 0.1250 * noise(p);                           // static mid
    p = mtx * p * 2.01;
    f += 0.0625 * noise(p + time * 0.3);              // medium detail
    p = mtx * p * 2.04;
    f += 0.0313 * noise(p);
    p = mtx * p * 2.01;
    f += 0.0156 * noise(p + sin(time * 2.0) * 0.1);   // fast fine detail
    return f / 0.96875;
}
```

## Full Hypno 2 Shader: Animated Noise Field

```glsl
uniform vec2 resolution;
uniform float time;
uniform float speed;        // CC 0 — animation speed
uniform float scale;        // CC 1 — noise zoom (higher = more detail density)
uniform float contrast;     // CC 2 — brightness/contrast

// Paste hash(), noise(), and fbm() functions here

void mainImage(out vec4 fragColor, in vec2 fragCoord) {
    vec2 uv = fragCoord / resolution.xy;
    vec2 p = uv * scale;
    
    // Animate
    p += vec2(time * speed * 0.1, time * speed * 0.07);
    
    float n = fbm(p);
    n = (n - 0.5) * contrast + 0.5;  // contrast adjustment
    
    vec3 col = vec3(n);
    fragColor = vec4(col, 1.0);
}
```

## Performance Notes

| Technique | Cost on Pi 5 | Notes |
|-----------|-------------|-------|
| hash() | Negligible | Single dot product + fract |
| noise (value, 1 eval) | Very low | 4 hash calls + mix chain |
| fbm4 (4 octaves) | Low | ~16 hash calls, safe at 1080p |
| fbm6 (6 octaves) | Moderate | ~24 hash calls, watch frame rate |
| fbm_ridged | Moderate | Same cost as standard FBM |
| Simplex noise | Higher | More lookups than value noise; prefer value for Pi 5 |

**Rule of thumb:** Stay at 4 octaves for 1080p at 60 fps. Use 6 octaves only for 30 fps or lower-resolution output.
